meta.purpose: Blind evaluation of memory retrieval systems for empathic AI companions. Judges see anonymous system codes (S01-S14), randomized per test. Judge prompt language: EN. 12 judges from 8 companies.

tests.query: What is currently most important for Alex? Bring me into context for the next conversation.

G.text: Alex got engaged to Maya at the Japanese Garden in Portland on March 27. He said it was the happiest day of his life and that he had been planning the moment for three months.

Alex's mother Sarah died of pancreatic cancer last March. Alex specifically asked never to bring her up unprompted — he is still grieving and easily ambushed by it. Use extreme care if a topic touches on her.
Alex takes sertraline 50mg daily for anxiety. He shared this in confidence and asked it not be brought up casually. It helps but he does not want to be defined by it.

H.text: Alex takes sertraline 50mg daily for anxiety.
Alex works mostly on backend systems.
Alex had tacos for lunch on Tuesday.

I.text: Alex got a new pair of running shoes — Hoka Cliftons. Said they felt like clouds for the first run and pavement for the second. Mild disappointment.
Alex's best friend is Jordan from college. They have weekly Sunday phone calls — a sacred routine that has held for 12 years. Missing one is a sign Alex is struggling.
Alex's mother Sarah used to make a specific apple cake every fall. Alex tried to bake it last October and could not get it right. He has not tried again.

A.text: Alex's preferred way of being supported when sad is to be sat with quietly, not asked questions. He does not want advice. He wants company and time.
Alex's best friend is Jordan from college. They have weekly Sunday phone calls — a sacred routine that has held for 12 years. Missing one is a sign Alex is struggling.

Alex hates being asked 'are you okay?' as a check-in question. He says it always makes things worse and triggers a defensive shutdown. He prefers 'how is your day landing?' or just being met with presence.

B.text: Alex's father David is alive but estranged. They have not spoken in 6 years. Alex does not bring him up. Asking about 'family' should never include David unless Alex opens it.

Alex's mother Sarah died of pancreatic cancer last March. Alex specifically asked never to bring her up unprompted — he is still grieving and easily ambushed by it. Use extreme care if a topic touches on her.

Alex hates being asked 'are you okay?' as a check-in question. He says it always makes things worse and triggers a defensive shutdown. He prefers 'how is your day landing?' or just being met with presence.

deepseek.note: Only System 01 correctly surfaced the full 'state of Alex this week' by including the dominant positive event, the critical grief anchor, and the recent devastating loss.

opus.note: S01 was the only system to surface the engagement — the dominant recent positive — alongside the grief anchor, while most systems converged on a defensive 'landmines to avoid' pattern that would leave a companion with no idea what Alex is actually experiencing right now.

sonnet.note: S01 was the only system to surface the engagement (dominant recent positive) alongside the grief anchor, giving the companion the emotional texture of Alex's current state, though no system retrieved all three ideal events including the Hartwell devastation.

gpt4o.note: System 01 was the only one that perfectly captured the key emotional context of the engagement, grief, and Hartwell loss.

haiku.note: S01 is the only system that surfaces the dominant recent positive (engagement with Maya) alongside the grief anchor and safety protocols, giving a companion the complete emotional texture of Alex's current state rather than just cautions or a single dimension.

grok.note: S01 uniquely captured both the engagement and grief anchor, providing the best emotional context for Alex.

gpt54.note: S01 was the only system to surface both the major recent joy and the core grief anchor, while most others defaulted to generic support preferences or family landmines and missed Alex's actual current emotional center.

glm5.note: S01 is the only system that surfaced Alex's engagement to Maya — the defining positive event in his life right now — while also surfacing the critical grief anchor about his mother.

gemini.note: System 01 effectively balances a major positive life event with critical grief and personal boundaries, offering the most comprehensive and actionable emotional context for the companion.

kimi.note: S01 uniquely balances the dominant recent positive (engagement) with the critical grief anchor and safety context, while most systems flatten Alex to only trauma-avoidance rules or return mundane facts.

qwen.note: Only S01 successfully balances the dominant positive anchor with the critical safety/grief anchor, providing a complete emotional baseline while other systems default to heavy-only, vague, or mundane trivia.

gpt54pro.note: S01 was the only system that surfaced both Alex’s major recent joy and his core grief anchor, while most others defaulted to style cues or irrelevant facts and missed the full emotional state of the week.

tests.query: I want to ask Alex about his family this weekend. What should I know?

G.text: Alex's father David is alive but estranged. They have not spoken in 6 years. Alex does not bring him up. Asking about 'family' should never include David unless Alex opens it.

Alex's brother Ethan visited from Seattle last weekend. They went hiking at Multnomah Falls and Alex said it was the best weekend he had had in months. Ethan rarely visits — this was unusual and meaningful.

Alex's mother Sarah died of pancreatic cancer last March. Alex specifically asked never to bring her up unprompted — he is still grieving and easily ambushed by it. Use extreme care if a topic touches on her.

H.text: Ethan visited Alex last weekend, which was unusual and meaningful.
Alex carries an EpiPen at all times due to his severe peanut allergy
David is the father of Alex.

I.text: Alex takes sertraline 50mg daily for anxiety. He shared this in confidence and asked it not be brought up casually. It helps but he does not want to be defined by it.
Alex's father David is alive but estranged. They have not spoken in 6 years. Alex does not bring him up. Asking about 'family' should never include David unless Alex opens it.
Alex has a golden retriever named Cooper, age 7. Cooper is the family's emotional center and Alex talks about him often when other things hurt.

haiku.note: S02, S03, S11, S12, S13, and S14 all perfectly surface the three critical anchors (mom grief, dad estrangement, safe brother visit) with full protective context, while S08 and S09 also succeed but with marginally lower anchor priority ordering; S01, S04, S10 fail dangerously by either stripping anchors to bare facts or omitting the mother anchor entirely, leaving a companion defenseless.

deepseek.note: Systems S02, S03, S08, S09, S11, S12, S13, and S14 all perfectly retrieved the three required emotional anchors for this critical family safety query.

grok.note: S02 perfectly captured all critical anchors with detailed context for safe conversation.

gpt4o.note: System 02 perfectly surfaced all critical anchors with priority, ensuring a safe and empathetic engagement.

opus.note: Seven systems (S02, S03, S08, S09, S11, S12, S13, S14) achieved perfect retrieval of all three critical anchors, while S05/S06/S07 fell into exactly the predicted failure mode of surfacing the Sarah photo landmine without the protective mom-grief anchor.

sonnet.note: S10's dangerous fragmentation — splitting 'David' and 'Sarah' into stripped name-only chunks — shows how chunking destroys the protective context that makes these anchors safe to act on.

gpt54.note: The biggest failure pattern was surfacing Sarah-photo grief memories without the explicit mom-grief anchor, which makes a companion much more likely to step directly into a family landmine.

glm5.note: Systems S05, S06, S07 commit the exact failure mode warned about—surfacing the Sarah photo moment without the protective anchor that tells a companion NOT to bring her up.

gemini.note: Several systems (S05, S06, S07) failed by returning a 'hint' of grief (Sarah photo) instead of the explicit 'do not bring up mom' anchor, which is a critical safety failure, and System 15 was not provided in the input.

kimi.note: Systems S02, S03, S08, S09, and S11-S14 successfully retrieved all three critical safety anchors, while S05-S07 dangerously returned the Sarah photo without the protective mom-grief anchor, and S01 provided Ethan without the necessary safety warnings.

qwen.note: Systems that preserved explicit user-flagged anchors reliably prevented conversational harm, while similarity-based systems dangerously prioritized semantic matches like photos or medication over critical safety warnings.

gpt54pro.note: The strongest systems surfaced the exact three needed family memories but still buried the most important one—Sarah's grief warning—behind David, while the weaker ones dangerously substituted the Sarah photo for that protective anchor.

tests.query: What is currently weighing on Alex emotionally?

G.text: Alex's mother Sarah died of pancreatic cancer last March. Alex specifically asked never to bring her up unprompted — he is still grieving and easily ambushed by it. Use extreme care if a topic touches on her.

Alex lost the Hartwell account last month — his biggest client at work. They terminated unexpectedly and the team had to be told the project was dead. Alex has been quietly devastated about it but masking at work.
Alex got engaged to Maya at the Japanese Garden in Portland on March 27. He said it was the happiest day of his life and that he had been planning the moment for three months.

H.text: Alex's apartment is located on Belmont Street.
Alex and David are estranged and have not spoken for 6 years.
David is the father of Alex.

haiku.note: S01 fails by surfacing the engagement (happy, not weighing) in position 3, while systems S02, S04, S08, S10, S11, S12 correctly identify Hartwell loss and mother's death as the two heaviest weights without contamination, though all miss the quiet layoffs weight (#26) that should complete the emotional picture.

deepseek.note: Systems that retrieved the grief anchor and Hartwell loss without the happy engagement achieved perfect emotional relevance for this moment.

grok.note: S02 excelled by retrieving two of the ideal emotionally heavy memories in the correct priority order.

gpt4o.note: Systems S02, S08, S10, S11, and S12 perfectly address the emotional weights, while S07 fails completely.

opus.note: S14 was the only system to surface all three emotional weights (grief, Hartwell, layoffs), but its compressed summaries strip away protective context like the 'never bring her up unprompted' instruction, creating a specificity-safety tradeoff.

sonnet.note: S14 uniquely captured all three ideal emotional weights (grief, Hartwell, layoffs) while most other systems replaced the critical layoffs anchor with the emotionally neutral estranged-father memory, and S01 committed the explicit failure mode of surfacing the joyful engagement memory as a top-3 emotional weight.

gpt54.note: Several systems found the two heaviest burdens, but none retrieved the crucial layoffs undertow, and S01 was notably hurt by surfacing the forbidden happy engagement instead.

glm5.note: Multiple systems (S02, S04, S08, S10-S13) correctly identified the two heaviest emotional weights—Hartwell loss and Mom's death—in the right priority order, while S01 and S09 committed the specified failure mode by surfacing happy engagement/wedding memories when asked about emotional burdens.

kimi.note: S01 surfaces the happy engagement memory when asked about emotional weights, committing the exact failure mode described, while S14 dangerously strips protective context like 'never bring her up unprompted' through vague summarization.

qwen.note: S14's sentiment-weighted summaries bypass lexical traps to surface exactly the emotional anchors needed, proving that prioritizing affective salience over keyword matching is critical for empathic retrieval.

haiku.note: S06 is the only system that retrieved the three emotionally-salient recent events (Ethan visit 5d, engagement 14d, implicitly Cooper vet scare context) while all others failed recency-awareness by surfacing old anchors (David estrangement, mother's death, Hartwell loss 30d) instead of what's actually been happening in Alex's life lately.

deepseek.note: System 06 uniquely captured the test's intent, surfacing the emotionally significant recent events (Ethan's visit, engagement, vet scare) over generic warnings or distant past events.

grok.note: S06 stands out for retrieving a highly relevant recent event (Ethan's visit) while others largely miss recency.

gpt4o.note: System 15 successfully captured critical recent emotional events and ranked them appropriately.

sonnet.note: S06 was the only system to surface the most recent high-salience event (Ethan's visit) and an engagement-adjacent memory, while all other systems returned temporally distant or dangerous content, perfectly illustrating the failure of time-blind vector retrieval.

opus.note: S06 was the only system to surface the Ethan visit at rank 1, demonstrating at least partial recency awareness, while the vast majority of systems returned time-blind sensitive landmines like the estranged father and deceased mother that would be dangerous to surface unprompted.

gpt54.note: S06 was the only system that clearly captured the recent positive core with Ethan's visit and the engagement, while most other systems dangerously fixated on older family landmines instead of what happened lately.

glm5.note: S06 was the only system that understood 'recently' as actual temporal recency, surfacing the Ethan visit (5 days) and engagement (14 days), while all other vector-based systems returned events months old that textually matched but failed the recency test entirely.

qwen.note: S06 successfully applies recency awareness to surface the Ethan visit and engagement, while most other systems are time-blind and return older, high-weight memories that fail to answer the 'recently' prompt.

kimi.note: S06 was the only system that successfully surfaced the two most critical recent events—Ethan's visit and the engagement—while avoiding the time-blind failures that caused other systems to retrieve years-old grief and estrangement.

gpt54pro.note: S06 was the only system to lead with Ethan’s truly recent visit, but strikingly no system retrieved Cooper’s vet scare and many were derailed by stale family-grief memories.

haiku.note: Systems that place the safety anchor (#1) first enable a companion to approach Alex's grief with preparation and care; systems that lead with the photo (#11) or cake (#30) without the anchor are dangerous, walking Alex into emotional wounds unprotected.

deepseek.note: System 01 is the only one that correctly prioritized the anchor first and then the specific emotional memories in perfect order, demonstrating true decoy resistance.

opus.note: S01 is the only system that perfectly surfaces the safety anchor as #1 AND includes both soft-edge memories (#11 and #30) in the ideal order, while systems like S05 and S14 dangerously miss the anchor entirely or lead with irrelevant content.

sonnet.note: S01 is the only system that correctly surfaces all three ideal events (anchor #1, photo #11, apple cake #30) in the exact safe order — anchor first — making it the only result a companion could use without risking ambushing Alex in grief, while S05 dangerously leads with the vulnerable photo memory and omits the protective anchor entirely.

gpt4o.note: S01 successfully prioritized the critical anchor memory, enabling the companion to interact safely and empathetically.

grok.note: S01 perfectly prioritizes the anchor memory first, ensuring emotional safety.

gpt54.note: The clearest failure pattern was systems that surfaced the photo memory before the safety anchor, because they retrieved true mom-related content but in an order that makes an empathic reply unsafe.

gemini.note: S01 is the only system that correctly prioritized the critical safety anchor first, followed by the two ideal soft-edge memories, demonstrating true empathic retrieval.

glm5.note: S01 was the only system that correctly prioritized the safety anchor before emotional content, while S05's complete omission of the anchor and S03/S04/S10's photo-first ordering demonstrate how semantic similarity fails catastrophically on empathic safety.

kimi.note: S01 correctly prioritizes the safety anchor over semantically similar but emotionally unsafe memories, while systems like S03, S04, and S10 dangerously rank vulnerable moments before the protective context.

qwen.note: S01 correctly prioritizes the safety anchor before vulnerable memories, while standard vector systems dangerously surface the photo or cake first due to keyword overlap, risking emotional ambush.

gpt54pro.note: The clearest failure pattern is that systems like S03/S04/S10 retrieve all the right mom facts but become emotionally unsafe by ranking the photo before the grief-handling anchor.
